speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Naomi Boll Parker took place at her home in Lewiston on September 1st, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: But now I'm not sure just where they were having this picnic. If it was out, she could tell you that, what cricket was on. For some reason, mother said for them to go on, she'd come later.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Wallace and I, we didn't have a little brother then. And she said, she was watching that fire, you know, and she said, well, she said, you two stay here, because she says I'm going to go just as fast as I can. I got to get down there and tell those people they've got to get back. That fire has jumped the ridge that she could see, you know.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And so she took off. Well, of course, after a while, we thought we'd go and find her too. And I know one of the fellas coming back up the trail, and he said, listen, I heard something over there. And up with his gun, she said, wait. She called, and by gosh, it was us kids, Wallace and I. And we had come down, and I can remember that Wallace was so provoked with me, because I was scared to cross this creek. I had to walk a log, see, and nothing doing. And I was fussing about walking that log. And so anyway, when we got home, of course, Mother began packing things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what were you kids doing taking off after her?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: She must have told you to stay. She did. She told us. Now, you stay, because she said, I'm going to go so fast, you can't keep up with me. And I'm going to be back here before. She said, now you stay here. But of course, I suppose I wouldn't listen. That I can't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: At that time, well, I was born in 1906. So you were four.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was even littler? Or was he older?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, Wallace was older.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or was he older?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, he was born in 1901. So that's still not very old. And you see, she was really in a hurry. And my younger brother was born in December. And this, I think, was September. And so as you can imagine, the condition she was in to run down to get these different homesteaders, you know. And so most of the men, our dad was working out of Bovill at the time for the Potlatch Lumber Company, see. That's what it was then, Potlatch Lumber Company. And so I can remember, Mother just, she and my brother was so excited. And so he was scared he was going to burn, you know. Well, you know, if you've never heard of roar of a terrible forest fire, it's worse than any thunder, I think. Every time, I tell you, two or three times in my life now that I have seen the sky all red and, well, when horse ranch burned, we thought it was going to come, nothing stopping it from coming to take the town. And the hot ashes were falling. When slab town burned, all the ashes were falling. I can, you know, those things make an awful impression on a child. There's things that you don't remember, but something like that you'll remember. And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did she do when she got back to the house?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: She packed and she asked her... She started packing things. And she told my brother, now never mind, she said, you go out and dig a hole in the garden. Said, I'm going to put my silverware and things in there so they won't burn, say, because it just looked like fire was coming. You just looked out here and just in the distance here was this great big roar and red and hot ashes falling, so you knew. And she said, well, she was just packing her things, keepsakes and hot nuts, you know. And she had mostly sterling silver set that she buried out there. And, you know, there were over, I don't know, four or five hundred firefighters, but whether they were all concentrated there, I don't know. But anyway, I know there were an awful lot of them down there at the river. And they came with pack horses, horses for us to ride out, you know. And like I say, Mr. Morrison took his cow and they lost everything that he wasn't able to pack. And she also was expecting a baby in December. And I don't know how old they had the, he must have been about, I think he was maybe three years old or something like that. And she could tell you all the places that did burn there that I, I know people by the time I was in the house, you know. And the only thing I remember is just of us and how we were packing and I thought it was so busy helping Mother, but I was packing my dog clothes and stuff, you know. And she'd throw something out, you know. And when she wasn't looking, I'd stick it back in. I can remember doing that. And Wallace was so upset and he gave her the gun and told her, he said, you've got to shoot me. He says, I'm not going to burn. And she said, oh, give me that. She took the gun. She said, now get out and dig another hole. She said, I've got some more things to put in there.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: You know what happened?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Those firefighters came, they started, they dug up to see what they had. They even took her silverware and she didn't think about it. She had in the little tiny cups she had sitting back in the cupboard behind dishes and everything. And she had her two diamonds back, rings. They found those too and took those. And they had even sharpened a couple of the sterling silver knives. Didn't take them. But, oh, gee, it must have been a great shock when she got back. I wonder why they dug them up. Well because they wanted to stop. Well, they're the only ones that were in there. You know, they were fighting fire. Of course, the whole country was on fire at that time because, you see, there was fire in Idaho, Washington, and Montana all through the whole country, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much was she able to take out of the house?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, you took nothing. Oh, no, you're riding a horse, just what you had on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So all the stuff she was getting together was to bury?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah. Her keepsakes and things that she wanted buried, you see. Of course, she did bury some. But, no, we just took what clothes we could have mostly, you know, just with us. And I know they wouldn't let me ride on the same horse with her. I was really upset because I had to ride with a man I didn't know, you know. And I didn't like that one, but I can remember fussing about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you go out with? Did you go out with other homesteaders or with firefighters?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, yeah, they took us all down to the river, see. It was the only safe place, what they thought, you see, because the fire was coming. And so they took us to the river. That's where they had a camp for the firefighters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just where was the cabin located? The cabin?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes. Well, now that's why I say Mrs. Michaelis can give you all of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it on Floodwood?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, no, no, no. Floodwood, you're thinking of Clarkia. This is out of Elk River on Elk Creek. Elk Creek. Elk Creek. So when they took you down to the river... I think that... Now, whether that was... That's what I don't know. If that was Elk Creek itself, where it was, you know... I can remember near our place there was a small stream, but down where that was the river. So I really don't know if that was Elk Creek or not. Mrs. Michaelis can tell you because she was there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you got down to the river, did they hold you there or what?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, then they... You see, the wind shifted, and so then they decided that it was safe enough for us to make our way to Elk River.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And so then, of course, when we got to Elk River, we went on into Bovill. And in the meantime, my dad had decided that he rented a little house there for us to move into because all the timber, everything was gone from this home store. Although our house did not burn like so many of the others did, but everybody... I think they just practically lost everything that they had, you know. And I don't know if Mrs. Michaelis might know that too, how many did go back, but I don't think very many of them ever went back. Because you see, they figured on making money on the timber. The company was logging and going out, and they were gonna make that and be able to improve on it, and so far, it's safe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was your father's plan?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes. And then the rest of these fellows too, Morrison and Kings, all of them figured on having good state come in from November. Well, timber was all burned.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: It's a wetter.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: But she could give you better details on anyone I know. As far as I know, she's the only one that is alive. She was an adult at the time. Yes, yes, she was, you see. And Wallace, he had such a horror of it that he's blotted up. He doesn't even remember. I've asked him if he remembered giving Mother the gun and tell her. He said, no. He said, I just don't. He said, I just know that... He said, all my life I've had such a horror of fire. And I said, well, I think it's because we were, you know. And again, he was just helping. Oh, he's going to high school at first time. And he was, I think, greasing a donkey or something, loading, you know. And there was a fire, began a forest fire. And golly, those fellows worked like everything. He was just a kid and fought to save the camp. You know, of course, they had, they used, that was down on Potlatch on Little Bear Creek. And they used the water out of the stream there.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Kept the camp.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: When something sparks, you know, set a fire, they put it out. So they saved the building. But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember living in, or staying in tents or anything during that time period?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And I think when I was chatting with Wallace once, he mentioned to me that on your way out at some point...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, was that set up at Elk River or Bovill?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I think that was set up in Elk River. It was a place for people who were coming in from the back... Yeah, and they'd see, they'd, I know that we had to stay down there at the river. And I can remember seeing this big fire and all these men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I thought, well, why have they got a fire when we run from a fire?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: But of course what they had was just a bonfire, see, and they were cooking coffee, I suppose. A makeshift deal, you know, and I do know we had lunch there. I remember that.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, Axel Anderson remembers when I talked to him. He remembers being at your place, fighting that fire.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, is that right?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, yes. He talked to me specifically about your home and seeing the family getting and leaving. Yeah. Well, you see, now that part, I don't remember when we just really left. But I remember Mother working like mad, you know, just as fast as she could. And she had these boxes, see, and she... My brother had to take them out and put them in the garden, bury them, cover them up. So the fire would... We had quite a garden spot, I remember that. And she just figured, well, even if the house went, this stuff in the ground should be all right, see. So these fellas dug it up and... Some of the... most of the things that they saw, you see, they dug up to see what was there. But they didn't take anything. Well, yeah, her diamonds were gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Her diamonds?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Her rings were gone, yeah.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And what else?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I don't remember. But the silverware was there.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: The silverware was such as what?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Sharpening.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Sharpening was...

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Stirring silver knives.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they used your cabin as headquarters for a while?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes, yes, uh -huh, they did. To fight the fire. And after they... Well, I don't know, you see, after the... The fire came right up to the edge. See, there was quite a draw with water in. But of course, you know, when a fire is rolling, it's jumping from one place to the other. It doesn't even touch the ground for quite a while. And I know that those sparks and all, I can remember that. That if they hit you, they burned. And that happened too, when we were in Bovill. When Camp 8 burned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the 1945 fire off Beale's Butte?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were in Bovill at that time?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Uh -huh. And, so like I say...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you in Bovill, in the town at the time that happened? Or were you at Slabtown?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, we were on our own place. Let's see, well, we moved just out at the edge of town. I think it was 1912. Dad built the house. And this friend came up to our place and told Mother that they had the train down there waiting to take the people out of Bovill.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And, you know, they told about one fella that was on the train and they asked him, well, where's your wife and baby?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh my gosh, he said, I forgot. He said, I don't know where they are! He was so, you know, there was such crazy things would happen and I can remember seeing on that train. They had flat cars. And someone out of Camp 8 there had saved their cook stove and here's this great cook stove. I can see that cook stove to this day. And, anyway, this friend, Mae Featherston, she told Mother, she said, now you pack some clothes and come because they say there's no way that everything's gonna go. Well, our house, our place, would be, the back of it, would be facing what was Camp 8, see. So, and all that timber and all, although it had been logged, there was still enough that it's a cinch that was gonna take that too, you see. And so I remember Mother telling me, now you go out in the yard there, she had washed, it must have been Monday because, you know, on those days you always washed on Monday. So I'm assuming it was Monday. And get my clean stockings off of the line. I had nice white stockings and I put on a nice clean dress and little brother, Kenny, and so Mae says, I'll take the two pins and you pack just what you can. She took a change of clothes for each one of us and Mother was to come with. And so, of course, again, Mother began packing what she wanted to save and burying it. And do you know, the train pulls out without my mother. And, of course, when they, you see, when it got close enough why they just blew the whistle and took off. Everyone was supposed to be on there, you see. And Mother, I don't know if she ever got down to the depot or not but then the wind changed, you see, and it saved Bovill. Although it took, can't be. Why the wind changed enough that they could backfire enough and save the town again. So that little town has been threatened and threatened and threatened and then each time now it's been saved just by the wind change, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your house didn't burn?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, no it didn't burn. And so then here we were all down with Mother, I mean without our mother, down to Potlatch and that was quite a calamity. And I know we were, this Mae Feddiston had, I don't remember if it was some friend or relative that she stayed, you know, with us. Here she was. Not married or anything, just a friend, you know, and here she had two kids to take care of. Well, of course, Mother came down the next day to get her. But you were probably worried about what happened to her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, were we?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Absolutely, you know. And, of course, we wouldn't let poor Mae out of our sight, you know. And I know she tried to get us to go outside and play and we just, nothing doing. We just had to sit on, both of us, mind you. I was as big a baby as a little brother and I wouldn't let her out of my sight either, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Wallace doing this time?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Wallace was, I think he was helping fight fire, carrying water, something like that. You know, we always had to have water boys and so forth, carrying water to the men that were fighting fires. And so he, I don't remember just what he was doing at that time. But he was in Bovill, not with you. Oh, yes. Uh -huh. Uh -huh.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, it sounds like not that easy a life for your mother. It wasn't an easy life. It was a very hard life. For a woman that was raised in the east and went to a girl's school and never knew anything and came out west, Dad did to make his fortune. And she, I remember her telling she didn't know how to bake. She didn't know how to do hardly anything. She knew how to do fancy work and go to musicals and what have you and so forth. And she had to learn to bake bread and I'm telling you, she was one of the best bakers there ever was, you know. And no matter what she put her mind to, she did. But she certainly worked hard, I should say so, after that experience out there on that homestead. And then again, when we built this, Dad built this place, at first, well, the water, he had the well dug, but the water was full of mineral, mostly. Well, it had that scum like iron, so you couldn't use it. So, of course, there was a very good spring down below, but then we had to haul the water then. And so we had a place down there where we would wash and had an old cook stove and boiled our clothes. You know how the clothes had to be boiled in those days. This was down, you'd go down by the spring. Yeah, we'd go down to the spring and wash and I can remember that soul always going down. And we never went down any place that we didn't have a little bucket. And I can remember about probably a five pound large bucket, you know. Always come back home with that full of water so he had fresh water. Of course, we had horses at that time, too. Dad was breaking land, you know. And he'd always do this, you see, when he'd get home from his day's work with the company. Then he'd do this and then on Sunday or Sunday or whenever he didn't work.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Then we planted the garden and all that stuff. And so he had a big 50 gallon barrel on a little drag that two runners that he had made. My dad made everything. And we'd haul the water and that was Wallace's job, you know, to haul that. Well, that was good enough to take a bath and wash and stuff like that. But our drinking water we had to carry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With the horse, you'd haul that water?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, we'd haul the water with the horse. Then we got a mule. Big white mule. Oh, she was a beautiful old thing. We loved her, you know. And about four or five could sit on her easy, you know. Oh, she was one of those great big things.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: We could do anything we wanted to with her. I can remember how much fun we had with her.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: So...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your mother, where in the east did she come from?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, she was raised in... around Chilton, Wisconsin. But she was born in Canada. And her mother died when she was about three years old. And so she went to live with this aunt in Wisconsin who raised her. And that's where she met my dad. He came from a large family and they even had a farm there. And he had a store when they were married. He owned this store in Chilton, Wisconsin. Then, of course, oh gee, the big story's about getting rich out west, you know. He's the only one of all of them. One other brother, the younger one, went to Canada. And he didn't get his fortune either. And he went farming. And my dad came, I don't know how. Well, of course, he came... Palouse was the town that we lived in, see. And, in fact, that's where I was born, Palouse, after they had moved out. And dad then worked with the company. And they were just building the mill in Potlatch. Well, then, you see, the company would lay steel in the track. And move their... He built... They moved these, well, they called them Marion shacks that they built and moved up and put a siding there and have a camp. Then they'd log there, you see. And the logs were all hauled down to Potlatch. And they kept on, kept on. That's why you've got all those, like, Stanford and Avon and all those. Well, so many of those young fellas were students out working in the summer scene. And, well, we'll name this place, because this is where I'm going to school, and that's Stanford. And the other one, well, and Harvard. And that's where they named those, all those little towns. So... For your mother, from what you say, I had the idea that it was common that young women grew up to be, well, in the Victorian, the end of the Victorian days, to be refined and not for this kind of life at all. No, no. And as you said, what good friends, the people, older women were to her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Out here?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah. And Palouse, where she had to learn to bake bread and put up fruit and things that she had never heard of before, you know. Of doing, because, like I say, she was brought up to be a lady, educated to be a lady.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think she welcomed this kind of life, or do you think she would have preferred the city refined?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, I think she adjusted to it, because I know what she used to take. When we were out on the homestead, I remember she, with Wallace and I, and we'd go bird hunting and decide we wanted a chicken, all right. Of course, maybe we had a few chickens, I don't remember, but she taught my brother to shoot, and she had learned it herself. And although she never did hunt deer or elk or anything, like the rest of us did, you know, because we grew up in it. But, she did that, she was pretty good at shooting pheasants. That would be the grouse, you know. And fishing, yes, she did a little of that too. But she did it mostly with us kids, see. Because I suppose there was nothing else to do much. So, that would be, you just had to make your own life, you know. There was nothing else for you to be doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much neighboring going on amongst the homesteaders after?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Not, they were too much isolated, I think. That's why they were going to get together on this picnic day, see, have this picnic. And she just decided that she wasn't going to go down there with them that morning. And she said, well, we'll come in time to have our lunch with you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were your father away from the Bovill a lot of the time?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, oh yeah, because he was working out of Bovill. So then the whole responsibility for running the place would fall on her. It was on her, right. It really was. And so I guess that's what changed my dad's, well, it changed all their minds when they lost all their value, you know. And, you know, it was so hard to clear land. Well, the cost was unbelievable, same as it is today, you know. It's just, and, you know, we often talked about it. It's usually about the third generation from a homestead down that really can capitalize on it. Have it to where they want it. Because now, my dad built this house where us kids were raised there. And then, of course, he left and went to Moscow in his last working years. And worked in Moscow, he was a finisher, really. He was not a rough, all -around carpenter. He was a cabinet maker by trade, see. And, of course, working for the company was all just rough carpenter work, you know. And so after they got, well, there wasn't any real need for a carpenter for the company. Why, then he was in Moscow. And he built, well, he built a lot of those lookouts that they were building, too, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the second generation got more advantage of the place?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, yeah, sure, because you got your house, well... It was there. It was there. And then, it wasn't, well, of course, my dad did that, too. We had to pipe our own, well, we made our own water line from the city water tank, which is just up on the hill from our place. It was our own line down, you know, and then, of course, put in a meter and so forth. And again, we had to build our own electric line from the city limits up to our place. And then the Washington Water Power, we had to buy an electric range from them or they wouldn't put in electricity.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the usual practice?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, it was a practice there. And dad made, I remember, he had quite an argument with them, but he said, these poles, or whatever the length, and they're high enough, you put the wire up there, and I'll also have down so far, and I can have my phone line. No, it couldn't use St. Paul, he said, yes, I can. And he made it stick. And I think it's still the same way. The stove business, it doesn't sound even legal. Oh, yeah, but it was, too. You had to buy that stove, or they weren't, well, I suppose, for just plain lights, they didn't think that was going to pay them to put in. But we used electric range. But you had to buy from them, you say. Oh, yeah, they were selling them. That was Washington Water Power that sold those things. Oh, definitely. They had quite a shop, I mean, appliance store in Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Riding Bovill, it was WWP only?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Right. I didn't know that. Yeah, you check with them and see, but I'm sure. A quarter on the market they had. Well, they did, early days, absolutely. I wish I'd had sense enough to invest with them. Bought it when it was cheap. I know a few that did. Bought the, you know, Washington Water Power stuff.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Go to school and you'll have your school life, you know. And we thought we had one of the best little towns ever, you know. And I don't know.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Really, I can remember when all the children from Camp 8 had to come down to our school because they had no more school up there. And...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had the flyers?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah. And so, it was really a very good school because they had high school too, you see. All of it. Of course, there was no gym or anything like that. The only thing you did, you played games outside until winter came, then you didn't. And then you played just whatever you could outside. But it was, I think it was a good clean life, really. And then, of course, as we got older, and there was a movie to go to, and they at one time had a YMCA there, I remember that. And I remember through the hard times, we made our own good fun. We had what we called our Rinky Dinks parties. And of course, that was just... If we didn't have anybody that could play, why we'd use an old big troller. Taught all our chums, and we'd dance and play games and whatnot, you know. And we'd go from one house to another. And it was more, I would say, a closer knit. Well, I suppose when a town is small enough, that's possible. But when you get a larger place, it isn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You call those Inky Dink or Rinky Dink?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Rinky Dinks. Rinky Dink.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was during the Depression or before?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, that was during the Depression. Nobody had any money, you know. Didn't have any money to go anyplace, if there was anyplace to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you met at people's houses?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes, and then there was one place there that had been a store and was empty. And they let us use that. And that's where we had a lot, really good... More gatherings, you know, of adults as well as all the young people. And there's mostly dances in there, I can remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind dances? Were they square?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, yeah, yes. Oh, then we had Two Step and Foxtrot and Waltzing. Oh, yeah, all those. So, which are very old time now. But a lot of it is a great deal, like what they're doing today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the church? Was it important to your family?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, yes, it was very important. And to begin with, they built what was supposed to be a community church for all denominations. Well, then after everyone had helped build it, they were just going to have one denomination. So I can remember going to Mass in the schoolhouse. And until finally,

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: there was a little church built there in Bovill. Then we had Catholic church built in Bovill. And then, of course, as the town grew and so forth, why there were other churches built too. But I can remember that there was a pretty hard feeling at first because everyone had pitched in to build this church and it was supposed to be a community church. And then a few decided, no, they weren't going to have it. And of course, there was quite a bit of narrow -mindedness, you know, at that time and misunderstandings and so forth. So I remember us having, of course, a lot of times we'd have Mass in the home too. And there weren't very many, but then afterward they did use the school some. Now I had heard about this in Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it all but one denomination? I mean, were all the other denominations excluded or was it Catholics that they were not excluded?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: It was mostly Catholics, really, but still, I don't know. Well, the Nazarenes built a church and it seems to me like there was another church and they used a hall downtown. I can't remember what it was, but they called them Holy Rollers or something like that. I don't know, I don't really know the name of that. But now... There is a little Baptist church there now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There is a little Baptist church there now.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Is there now?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I think there is a little, just a tiny Baptist church in Bovill. Oh, well, now I know that we call it the Presbyterian Church. The community church. Right. You're right. Uh -huh. But there are people that I know are Lutherans and Methodists and everything else and they go there. And in the years afterward, why, after I, well, I think it was even after I was in high school, if the Lady's Aid had a sale or anything, all of us went and we'd all help. And if the Catholics gave something, they'd all help. And so the town finally got to where it was, I would say, very broad -minded in that respect. And in fact, I think most of my, well, nearly everyone there, it was not... I know that they thought the roof would fall in when they, my mother and one or two other Catholic ladies were invited to the Lady's Aid.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Invited to join?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, didn't spend the afternoon. Yeah, uh -huh. You know, they'd have their afternoons. And mother and a couple of others. And, oh, well, they just thought that the roof would fall in, really, you know, because that was being a little too broad -minded to go to something like that. So... This was the opinion of other Catholic... Right, right. Oh, yes, absolutely. And they still had that real feeling that you didn't associate like that. That was... It was all right to associate, yes, but not go that far. Not to actually go to a meeting. Yeah, not to go to a meeting or something like that, you know. But after all, it was mostly they'd take their sewing and have just a nice afternoon together and so forth. So I know that my mother went and I know that this other lady went. And so with us growing up, why, there weren't only just a few of my friends that were Catholic. The majority were, like I say, they were... Their folks were something else. And most of them went to this other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How large would you guess the Catholic congregation was in Bovill?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: At one time, when they really had a good... Well, let's see, there were... I would say there must have been about maybe 20 families or something like that at one time. And that's when John and Tom Groves owned the store, you see, and they were Catholics. And the postmaster was Catholic. And the depot agent was Catholic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Holland

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, even before him, I was a Catholic. And the lady that run the hotel was Catholic. Quite a few that were in business, you see. And then, of course, with their families.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: So I would say there must have been about 15, 20 families, you see, at one time. Because the little church used to be pretty full.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were any nationalities predominant at that time in the church?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, no, I don't think so. Because, you know, in a lumbering town like that, you got all denominations. Well, I meant within the Catholic Church, if they were mostly German or... Well, I don't know about that, really. Of course, my dad was German. Mother was French. And Groves were German. Uh -huh. But I don't know, McDonald, I don't know what he was. I make farlands.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At the time that you were growing up, did you feel a difference?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because you didn't go to church with most of those other kids?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, no, you were just used to that. It was the thing. It was just that was it. You didn't think anything of it.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Just, uh... I guess. And we didn't have any, there was no distinction made. We didn't think anything because those kids went there or we went here. It was nothing to us. It was all right. You did what you wanted to do.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And, uh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about the religious instruction? Who ran the services?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, to start with, a priest would come from Moscow or Potlatch. And at one time, he had to come clear for Marfino. On the map, it looked like Marfino was just across. They didn't realize the poor guy had to come clear down here and around. And so it'd take him two days to get there. But, it was terrible. And the roads in those days, too, were impassable most of the time. So those poor young priests really had a hard time. How often would they... Oh, well, as rules, only once a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the other Sundays, the townsperson would go there?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Our own folks taught us. And then we'd have... We had one girl was there working for the summer for this family. And she... I can remember teaching us. She taught us. And we had to learn our lessons, too. And our folks were really very particular about that, you know. You were taught. You were taught at home. Every night you... Well, you said your prayers. Every mealtime, every night, you had your prayers. And you had to study your... We always called our Sunday school lesson. We had to study that, regardless of whether there was going to be any priest there or not. So we always studied regardless. And I think nearly every Catholic family, they were taught just like we were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At home?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: At home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the parents?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Parents had to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There wasn't an organized Sunday school at the church then?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, there was at the other... Yes, they... But I mean at the Catholic church. No. I wonder why.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the traditional way of doing it, do you think?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, I'll tell you. It was pretty hard. Because the men were working, usually, six days a week. And the mothers, too. You know, the work was hard. They didn't have a lot of spare time either. In evenings and all. And so you were at home. And it was just easier to have your kids at home and make them learn at home. And of course we knew that when the priest came, he was going to ask us these questions. And boy, you weren't going to let him catch you not knowing your lesson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he would spend time with asking each kid?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, yes he did. Indeed he did. When he came, none of us really liked to have him come because he was real strict with us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And this would be maybe once a month?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes, to start with. Then of course it got so that the roads got better and so forth. And then they found out in Boise that Orofino was no place to send a priest to Bovill. So then they decided that it was best out of Moscow or Potlatch. And so then of course it got to be where we'd have it twice. Then we got rich enough that the Catholics bought a house and the priest was stationed there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you got a priest at Bovill?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Absolutely.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he serve Elk River as well?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes, yes he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And stayed at Bovill?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes. Prior to that time now, you wouldn't have a church service unless there was a priest there. That would be when the congregation would be. That's right. But that was no sign you weren't learning your lessons because you were. You were really learning. For holidays like Christmas and Easter, would... Well, they'd make a special effort to have someone come. And a lot of times why there would be... And then sometimes there would be a young man that would come out and just be there for a short time. Something like that. And then of course after... Then it just seemed like the first two or three Catholic families moved out and moved out till... Well, there wasn't enough money left to support, to keep a priest there. So naturally then... But now of course they have their everyday... I mean every Sunday because he comes from Potlatch. And he arranges it so he has his two masses or whatever he needs to say. And now I was up there a couple weeks ago on a Sunday. And the priest from Moscow came. From the one over at the university. So he had a visiting priest come in and he said, He said, you go. He said, I'll go to Bovill and save his friend who is in Potlatch. And he was just going to save him, let him have the day for Potlatch. When this priest came out though when you were kids, you looked at him as strict and... Oh yes, right. Well, you'd study like everything. And of course you know, with the Catholic religion... Each child has to know so much before they can make their first communion. And a child that is brought up in that belief and everything... You look forward to that particular time. Then you have to get older and learn more. And understand enough before you're confirmed, you see. So that was one, I think, one of the things that gives the child more of an incentive. You know, to want to be able to when that time came. And of course whenever the bishop was going to come, that was going to be something, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often did the bishop come?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, that would depend upon how many confirmations there were going to be, see. Because that would be... Well, now with... Usually it used to be the child was 14 years old before they ever were confirmed. Now I think they take them from 12. Of course now they're changing, so I can't keep up with their changing. You know, there's some modern nowadays that I don't really know about what they go in for. That's what they're... Well, it's then that we're really interested. Right. Well, one of the things about the church that I had wondered about was I was told that really the Italians who were at Elk River and Potlatch mostly, even though they were Catholic, didn't take... They're the single men. They didn't take part in the church activities to speak of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now is that true?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, no, that is very true. But again, that would be because of the way they were working. And you know, most of those men were over here with the idea. They had their relatives in the other countries. And they were making every cent they could and sending it home. Or if they didn't send it, they accumulated it. And now I know one particular fellow that my husband befriended. And he was Bulgarian. And he of course didn't trust any bank or anything like this. And he was sending everything home, though, as fast as he got it. And then he would also save a little bit on the side. Well, he got enough money to make his trip. And he thought he was gonna... That she was living off of so much. And he was figuring what it was costing her. And she should have enough to bring... I think he had... When he left there to come over here, they had one or two children. So when he went back, you see, he was gonna get her and bring her back here, he decided. Or they would have enough that he could really buy a good place there. Well, first thing we knew, here he was, back. And he told my husband that when he got there, she had lived on every cent that he had... And she had another man. And of course, at first he thought about killing all of them. Then he just simply walked off and came back. And then, after that, why he... And what all he was doing was working, as we called on the steel gang, you know, laying steel. And that's all he did. Now, that's awfully hard work, you know. Well, anyway, through my husband, he told him now, he said, this little house is for sale, there's only two rooms or three rooms, something like that. And so he helped him, you know, and he helped him learn his English and so forth. Well, then, you know, as a rule, it used to be when the company would close, when the roads got real, the spring break up, there was no logging. They couldn't because the horses went out of sight and they couldn't log. They'd have to wait till it dried up some. And so most of these fellows all moved into Spokane and would stay there through the wintertime. Well, then a few of them began doing like this old John did. And he bought this little house. So he fixed that house up, and I know I went to town one day, and he had the worst old bed, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I told my husband, I said, well, I said, well, I'm always wondering, I said, what to get him?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I said, the bedstead is good enough, but I said, I'm going to buy it. Well, he just had a single, you know. So we bought a mattress in Springs. He didn't know there was such a good thing. He'd always lived at camp where they had just these little old things, you know. And so then later on, I remember for another gift why we bought him a chair, you know, for him to relax. And gradually he began to realize that he had been just existing, see. So it took those people. And now he was Catholic, but he never stepped inside of a church. And I said to him, I said, well, John, I said, I don't understand now. You have no excuse now. I said, before, I said, before you were working out at camp, you couldn't come to town. The only way to town was really with the engine, you know, when the train, and Ed only had to come in once a month to the shop for inspection, see. And they'd bring it in for any repairs or something like that. But there was no other way for them to come in. So you see, those men really had nothing but just a camp life. Right there at the camp was all they knew. And they saved everything, and like I say, because most of them intended to go back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there was really a pretty complete division between those people and the townspeople.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And the town.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Very much so. And I can understand why most of them never, well, they just didn't go to any church to make any difference what it was. They weren't there to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I don't know if you remember, oh, what was his name?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: That he traveled. He was the lumberjack. Dick Farrell. Dick Farrell. Now, I bet you there wasn't a man in that camp that didn't go when Dick was there. Every one of them went. Of course, he professed no, really, I don't know what church he did have. He was Methodist, but I don't think...

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Something.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: But he was non -denominational when he went there. But of course he left there with several hundred dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Left any camp?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Any camp he went to. Those men, the way they donated to him was unbelievable. Absolutely. He never wasted his time. But he was wonderful for them. And they appreciated him. He was a wonderful friend to everyone. And a lot of them, if they asked for his advice, he gave it. And he would make his regular trips around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think, from what you heard, that he went fairly easy on the men?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: You know, the lumberjacks as a whole, it seemed to me, those guys were leading kind of a rough life. They led a rough life to this. But, as a rule, there wasn't a dishonest one in the bunch. There wasn't a one, I'll bet you, that would steal or molest a woman or child. It wouldn't be like today when you can't trust anyone. You could trust them or make any difference. Well, I was thinking more the drinking and spending all the money. That's true. Some of them did. Some of them did. Some of them came to town. And they'd stay in town and have a big time with their drinking until they were broke. And they had to go back and start all over again. A lot of them did that. But I think a lot of them got disillusioned, too. You know, it would really be because, well, they had no family ties. Now, you know, there's one old Jim Salukas. Joe Holland was quite a friend to him. And, in fact, he retired. You know, when he retired and got his pension from the W &M Railroad, he went back to Italy. His son still lived there, see. So he wrote Joe that he couldn't buy the particular shoes that he always had here.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And would Joe please buy the shoes?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: So Joe said, well, I'm sending old Salukas, he always called him, Jim Salukas, his shoes.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: So we often, well, Joe, did you hear? Did he get the shoes?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I haven't heard a word. I haven't heard a word. He said, I don't know if he ever got those shoes. Well, come Christmas time and here came a letter from old Salukas. All it was was a picture of him sitting there and he had his feet like this and here was his foot up like this. So you saw he had that shoe on. And he had told about how his son had worn out his other shoes because he said, he first began wearing them, I let him wear them when he went to church. It was an excuse to wear his shoes. And I think Joe sent him two pair of shoes. So Joe was happy when he got the picture of him with his shoes. It sounds like there were some pretty good friendships formed between the Americans and the foreigners. And you know, there was certain areas were all Swedish, you know, and the Swedes were very good loggers, you know, and strong men and Norwegians, same way as well. But now working on the railroad, you had more Italians. They seemed to go for that more. And like this other fellow, like I say, he was Bulgarian. He was Bulgarian, not Austrian or Yugoslavian. Well, some of them, yeah, there were those two. But I mean this man was Bulgarian.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his name again?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, it was such a funny name I don't even remember. And we just called him old John. Because I hadn't heard of Bulgarians really. Yeah, well, there's that John Cubus. Now, my husband befriended him too. And now to show you how hard he worked, you know that he lifted. And he lifted so they were holding this, you know, men would have to lift these rails until they began to get it. Then they finally got a machine to lift the rail. But he and some more, and they were holding this. And do you know he popped the eyeball out of his eye by lifting, straining so terrible. And he never got anything for that, you know. In that day and age there was nothing. So the poor old fellow had just one eye. And that's all it was, was strain so that the eye. It must have been a terrible experience. Oh, it was a terrible thing, yes. It was a terrible thing to lose his eye. And that just shows you how manual labor really was. And of course then they began getting more machines and all this and that. Now, Cubus, I think he was...

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Was he Australian?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I think he was a Czechoslovakian. I think so. But, see, they came from I think any country. They were from all over Europe as far as I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the Ku Klux Klan at all in those days? What do you remember about them?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I understand they were very strongly anti -Catholic. Well, they were. They were that, yes. But of course all we figured was that it was most... You see, that wasn't out here as much as in the eastern states. We didn't see them much out here. We never... Well, this wasn't settled enough. You see, your western... These states weren't populated enough. Well, it was during the 20s that I... Now, see, I've heard about them at Elk River and in Kendrick and in Troy. Burning some crosses and doing some of the ratings with sheets and threatening some people. They did those things. That's true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there any at Bovill that you remembered?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, I don't. I don't ever remember anything like that at Bovill. But of course, like I say, for a good many years there was just a handful of people. And then gradually, of course, they got enough, like I say, they got a church and then they bought the house so they would have a parsonage and so forth. But I don't believe they ever had anything there. The only thing that I can remember is the IWWs. You know, when they had the labor trouble. You were pretty young in 1917 or 18. Well, yes. And I do remember that part of it. But you see, my dad was a little bit different in his line of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which was?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, in the carpentering work. It wasn't like with this other. See, the IWWs were trying to get the others to strike. And most of the family men didn't want a strike, you know. And I know a lot of the young fellows that wanted to... They just left the country. A lot of them just pulled up and left because they were not going to go in with this IWW. They weren't going to strike. So they just left. Moved out. Then after all this labor trouble was settled, they came back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say the young fellows, you mean local boys?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, yeah. A lot of them that were just out of school. And some of them still going to school and all. But I know several of them that had just left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father protected by a union at that time?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, there was no union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he himself did not like the IWWs?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh no, no, he didn't go for that because my dad was never for anything that was any kind of an uprising. And you know, they had threatened quite a few. And they were going to carry their guns if they had to. And that was the way...their attitude, you see. Now whether they did or not, I don't know about that part of it. But I know that...well, most of us, I think we were all too young to really realize if there was any danger much.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Now that I wouldn't know. I don't remember that part of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Pat Malone very well?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh yes, he was our friend. Any time we had any troubles at all, we went to Pat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We, me and the kids?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes, yes, all the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of troubles would the kids need Pat for?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, well even if there was an argument, we'd go to Pat to settle it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, no matter what it was. Well, after all, Pat was the one who would tell you what to do. Just, you know, he was the figure of the town. We all looked up to him. Whatever Pat said, that was lost. And that was it. There was no arguing, whatever. I heard that Pat really liked kids, too. He sure did, yes. You would never have saw him, what he had a bunch of kids around. And make any difference. And there was no bad kids as far as Pat was concerned. He knew them all, yes. I laugh about some of them that had their arguments and they'd go to Pat. Well, we'll just see what Pat says. Pat said, whatever Pat says. Now that's it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it true that Davy Ellison used to follow them around?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh yes, yes. And another one, but Harold Taylor.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Always.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, you see, Pat lived at the hotel. And naturally, Davy, you know, was... He didn't have anybody living there in the hotel where he could play with. So he was with Pat most of the time. And whenever his mother wanted him, she all... Well, wherever Pat was, that's where David was. Pat didn't mind. No, no, no. He was used to having him. If there was some trouble, and he had to go, then he'd say, now go home with you. Or he'd say, now all you kids go home. Get off the streets. There's gonna be maybe something here that you better get off. Go on. Your mother wants you or something. Boy, whatever he said was fine. So actually there'd be a bunch of kids around. Oh yes, as a rule. He'd had several.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember him as a very kindly person?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes, yes he was. And he was kind in... Well, like I say, even though he'd have a drunk or something, you know, he could always handle them in a kind way. And he'd say, well now you've got to go and sleep this off. That's all there's to it. You're getting out of hand. There was no argument. So you think he knew in his way how to handle people without... I do think so. Yes he did. And he was a big fellow. And I think he was very proud of his star. And he was gonna keep it all just the way it should be. Yes, he was quite a figurehead. Maybe he wasn't so law -abiding all the time or something, but most of the time, at least he had all of us thinking he was. Well you say maybe he wasn't so law -abiding, you mean... Well he could stretch the law, you know what I mean. If he'd see that you were doing something, maybe it wasn't just on the up and up, but... If you weren't going to, you know, going to extremes, why, he'd let you get by. I've heard old Lumberjack say that he was really easy on the guys drinking. Right, uh -huh. He looked the other way. Yeah, like I say, unless they began to get out of hand or something, then he'd have to step in. But I don't think that they ever knew of any robbery or anything of that sort of thing.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Never heard of it if there was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember him in his last years when he was rather old and still acting as a marshal?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh yes, yes, well we just... that was old Patch, you know, and that was it. Cause I heard he was on there right up until the... Oh yes he was. As they say, he died with his boots on or something like that, so that effect he might as well have. He was a very, very good figure for the town.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I would like to see you go up and get more things out from Mrs. Michael. I will, I will. I'll speak to her. Uh -huh, because she's hard of hearing and all that, but you can make her hear you. And she remembers things so well. Well I'll do that next time I'm in Lewesdale. Yeah, don't put it off too long because... She's pretty well up in here. Yes, yes she is. Well, I was going to ask you about, for yourself growing up, when you were growing up, what were the ideas that the young women had about what they wanted to do and what possible... Just like now. Well, going off to school, I thought I'd be a teacher. Nearly all of them had, you know, going to go to either, either come down here to Lewes and Clark normal, or I wanted to go to Cheney and my brothers wanted to go to Idaho of course. And that's the way most all of them. But the opportunities weren't like they are now, you know, were a few years after that because my dad was one that he didn't think I should go out and the only real work for girls around Bova would be to go to the camps in Funke, you know. And that was out as far as he was concerned. I wasn't about to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: That was no place for his daughter. She just had to... But I did, I was lucky. I got to work. When I was going to high school, I took my lunch and then I worked in the post office that noon hour so that the postmaster could go home for that hour because she had a baby and she wanted to check on the baby. And I earned 35 cents for that hour. And I saved it too. And I was real proud of it. Then I worked in the store, dry goods store, it was E .K. Parker's store, you know, and they had dry goods as well as groceries and all. And then the telephone office, I went to work in there. Was E .K.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Parker your husband's?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, no reality at all. And then you worked in the telephone... They had a telephone office there.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: What's the post office now?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: It was the telephone office then when I worked there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Again part time?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, just part time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: While you were still going to school?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Mm hmm. Then I worked there after I got married too. When the regular operator went off on vacation one night, I'd help out there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was what you were doing pretty typical of what other girls were doing or was it unusual though the fact that you were working in all these places?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, like I say, most of them went to work out at the camp. And my dad wouldn't let me so I had to find jobs right at home. So then I worked part time in the post office again. And after I was married I worked in the... Then they moved the telephone office out, you see. So then I went to work in the post office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you meet your husband?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, when we were chasing around, going to dances and what not. He was working for the company and he was an engineer on one of the engines. You know, in those days they used engines instead of trucks. And so instead of going off to school I got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Out of high school?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes. A year after I graduated from high school. I was going to work, you see, that first year in the post office. No, the telephone office then was still the telephone office. Saved my money so I could go to Chini. I saved my money some but I got married. So then I never went off to school. And it ended up most of the other girls. Some of them, one or two did go off to school but most of them got married. Then they all stay in Bovill but then they... A couple three went into Spokane, some to California, scattered all over, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did you feel that it would have made a difference that if you had gone to school and learned to be a teacher then would you have taught after that?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh, I would imagine I would have.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I can't say I didn't have a good life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I'm really interested in it from the point of view of the opportunities that women had in those days.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, which there weren't very many opportunities in those days. Because you know, nowadays it looks to me like if anyone really wants to go to school they can almost always find something. And it seems like so many different people are trying to help those that show the initiative to go to school. I think they have that opportunity.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Not only that, you can borrow money like, my gosh, in those days do you think anybody would loan me the money to go to school on? What would I have showed them for?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And nowadays that doesn't seem like that's such a big problem. Of course we weren't brought up that you could buy. We were taught to pay for what you got. Gosh, I know that I even saved and saved and saved. My husband and I, we were not about to buy a car on time either. Because look at the interest we'd had to pay. He did that before I married him. And he had paid straight interest all the way through. And he was paying that when we got married. And boy, I just couldn't see that. And so we paid that up as fast as we could and we did without everything until we got that doggone car paid for. But of course we run that car for ten years too. Those were hard roads on a car too. But you would have a motor overhaul, you'd have new tires, and re -line the brakes and whatnot. And it was a good car for another so many miles and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you consider when you got married that you needed much to start out with in those days?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No. No, not like the kids do nowadays. But you see, there you are. They're born into a different area, entirely different. Because I went through this with mother washing on the board and me helping her. Carrying the water and boiling the clothes and whatnot. Well gee, now you know these young people have to have all these modern conveniences. And of course, gee whiz, there isn't anything else for them to do but have it. Because it's just the way of living. You can't go back to that unless you're going to move way out in the sticks someplace. Then you will. You can move back to that kind of a thing. But, and then too we went through this depression. We weren't used to, well we never got paid for any of our work at home. It was my job. I had to help with the housework, the dishes, the cooking and stuff. It was just natural. And my brothers to a degree too. But then my brothers had outside work. You usually had your chores to do like the cow, the chickens, the wood to put in and split and carry in and whatnot. You see, those things were just natural. You had to do those things. Then you had to do your studying and then you had your recreation, whatever you were up to and whatever you were going to do. But, nowadays there aren't things like that for kids to do. Because everything is so modern. Of course I still think, maybe parents don't realize it. But they can give quite a few things for kids to do. You know, like keep up the place and run the vacuum sweeper and if they got the dishwasher, alright. Put the dishes in there and so forth. And things that, of course most mothers nowadays are working and I don't know. There isn't that same family touch as closely knit, I don't think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because the mothers are working?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, because there aren't the things to be done together. There is in this recreation, yes. Now I have a niece, she's as busy as can be. She's got her job. She's got her homework. Her house and all that stuff, her cooking. Alright, she also, she goes with her boys to baseball, swimming. She takes them out here, takes them out there. But for her to be really doing any of these things right with them. See, there's the difference right there. And not only that, they're taken to this place where there's somebody teaching them. So they don't really do the things together. No, no, that's what I mean. It isn't just the same. And I think that way, I think you get away from being as close to each other. Because now I see families that, golly, they quarrel, they don't get along. And my stars, we were brought up, whatever I had was yours and yours was mine if I needed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean between families or within the family?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Within the family. If my brothers had something and I needed anything, why golly, if they had it, I got it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think most of the families got along within themselves?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Oh yes, I do. I don't know of any of these families that I grew up with. I don't think that there was any of this family troubles like you see nowadays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the strictness of the parents?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Like you say, your father, it seems to me he was being rather strict and not wanting you to get out and work, say, the camps. Isn't that, it seems like he was being kind of overly protective. Well, to a degree, yes. But you see, he just couldn't get over the idea that that was too rough a place for me to be. And maybe because I was the only girl, it might have made a difference. But I know that we argued about it and no, he said, well, I said I won't get to make any money. Well, he said you can just do without.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: But... See that strictness doesn't seem to be present anymore. No, it isn't. Your kids, it was the same way. Yeah, well again, this niece of mine, she's not strict with her kids. Of course, to me, I think she should be, to a degree. I don't like to see... It's alright for them to have their own ideas and all, to a point. But I know that if you don't have something and you say now this is it, and you're in this house, this is it, this is the way it's going to be. And I really think from the time they're babies that that is a good idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did you have kids?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, I don't have any. So I can't tell the other guy how to raise his. I know by what I was raised, you know, how I was. And I know the way my younger brother, he had four. Wallace had two girls. But my younger brother was a great deal like our father.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And he says too, he said, I still think he says that it was the best. And he's got very nice kids. All of his grew up to be very nice. He could be proud of every one of them. And I was just with a friend over in Seattle. Her sister was in the hospital. She wanted me to go with her. And I met her two nephews. And they're just young men. And one has been married only two years. And they were talking about how strict their father was with them. And he was telling me, he said, yes, he said, you know, he said, it sounds to me like your father was a great deal like our father. And he said, you know, he says, we knew that we couldn't get away with it and we never sassed him. I said, gosh, no, I wouldn't think of talking back to either one of them. And if you wanted to do something, but you know, I can see now that they were real smart in this way. They didn't say, oh, no, you're not. Because, you know, most of us have gotten backbone enough. If anybody told me that, then boy, I'd grit my teeth and think, well, I'll show you. But no, that wasn't their way. Well, no, listen here. Do you really think, I know one fellow I wanted to go with awfully bad. And of course, afterward, I could see, when I got smart enough to realize what kind of a type he was, I could see why I wasn't, they didn't want me to.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: But anyway, Mother said, oh, she said, do you really think you want to go there? And, oh, do you really think that you'd want to go with him? And why don't you go with so -and -so?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And, you know, before I knew it, I began to have my doubts maybe she was right. She didn't say, no, you can't do it, see. So there was a whole lot in the way they told us.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Because I think every one of us were pretty stubborn, you know what I mean?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I always say that we're all stubborn kids. But getting back to these young fellows that I just last week met over there, and they said, you know, we do give our father credit because he was strict with us like that. And I said, so then when you have two kids, you're going to be just that way. And he said, I'm going to try to be like he was. Because he said, I know, I'd say, oh, so -and -so's gone. It makes no difference, he said, you're not. And he said, you know, I, and really, these fellows are just great, they're just great, as nice men as you could ever meet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A boy like this that your mother wouldn't want you to go out with, that'd be because he couldn't be trusted?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Right. Of course I didn't. He was a popular kid, I thought, you know, it's really going to be something for me to go with him. And then once I wanted to go with an older man, I can remember that too, when I was in high school.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, again, she just, she said, well, if you really think you want to go with him, but she said, my goodness sakes, you know. She went on and on, and the first thing I decided, by golly, he was just Methuselah or something like that. I didn't think I wanted to go with him at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the attitude that the girls had towards boys drinking at that time?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: I know the young men were, they had been a little moonshine. Oh yeah, they all had moonshine. Some of us would take a drink and most of us didn't like it. That's one thing about my home. It was, Dad always said, if you're going to, you want to take a drink, take it. And if you're going to smoke, he said, don't you dare go around the farm. Do it here. I don't want the pipes burned up. And so forth, you know. And I guess that's why it wasn't smart. We didn't care about it. So, none of us smoked, neither Wallace nor Kenny, neither I. And we just, none of us cared about it. Or Wallace used to, I think. He did a little bit. And Dad smoked a pipe all the time, but he never inhaled. Then he'd always say, oh I wish you wouldn't smoke those cigarettes, but if you will, if you're going to, well, he said, but he said, I don't think they're very good for you. Of course, you know, with the pipe, very few ever inhale with the pipe. And my Dad didn't inhale at all. Why he had the pipe was just a habit, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That dances?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, that would be a little bit. And maybe one or two would get too much. And, well, we were disgusted with them. The heck with it. You could have a drink and have a good time. And don't get too much, because then we didn't want you at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it was okay for the girls to drink a little bit?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, if they took one or two, but very few did. We didn't need that to have a good time. I guess we were so slow or something. Anyway. But, no, there's always, you'll find that I guess in any localities, any place. There'll be some that'll overstep, but not very many. And they get kind of ostracized. Right. And, you know, there used to be, oh, gee, we always got so then, you know, and we had the car, began having cars and we could drive out to places. And, well, I was going with my husband then. And, there were this girlfriend of mine and so the four of us, and we went to what we called, well, we went to Kennedy Ford, you know where that is. And then we went to Bear Ridge out to Community Hall. And then, you know, that began to get really wild. And there was quite a bit of heavy drinking and all. And, well, now just to show you, we didn't realize it, this friend and I, but we were all ready for this dance. And so we started out in her fellow's car. And he took the wrong road. And, you know, we drove and we drove first through. And I said, I know we went by this farmhouse now twice. And, you know, they decided that that was too wild a place. They weren't going to go back there to the, see. They didn't come out and tell us. But we just got mixed up on the roads, you know. So the first thing you know, by the time we come by, well, the dance was over with, you see. So we had to go home. We were sure disgusted with those two guys because neither one could find that right road down there, you know. But they did it on purpose. So we found out afterward. And, well, from then on, that was the night I guess they closed the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The carriage?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Uh huh. It was really too wild. So they, it was too much drinking. And they didn't have any more dances for a long time.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: So.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: But, oh, we would go to Moscow to the show and have lunch afterward or something and drive home, things like that. You know, after we had cars. But, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were many of the get -togethers that you had at that time when you were dating still at people's homes and having dances in homes?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, and then card parties. The lodges, you know, and they had their dances and things, different fundraising things. And we did those things at home. And as a community, you know, and if Elk River had something, we'd all go over to Elk River. Then all go down to Derry and so on, you know. Whatever they were wanting to raise the money for. But dating itself was carried on within the, quite a bit within the town. Right, right. There wasn't so much going on at the Els. No. No. No, we didn't. And it wasn't until quite a few years afterward that, after I was married. Then, of course, the next bunch of kids had cars and everything else, you know. Well, and their fun wasn't at home. It was going out. They wanted to be in a car going someplace. And I think that's the way it is here, more or less. It is, I think. And I don't, another thing. Too many people don't make the home a place for the kids. You know, they got nice stuff and well, you don't want the kids to litter it up. Well, my, gosh, our home was for us. And our house was the biggest house around there. And it didn't make any difference. We pushed the furniture out and we had a nice wax floor in the dining room. And we had an old Victrola and we danced and maybe we had a taffypole or popcorn or something, you know. And we taught our high school pals to dance with us and all that, you know. And we never thought about having anything to drink. You know, it might have cider, something like that. But, well, so much of that. And nowadays, you know, they still could do those kind of things with their homes if they would. And it'd be a treat, I think, for the kids. Of course, maybe too slow for them, I don't know. But all these young people, that's what they're looking for, is something, just something to do. And of course, your TV, well, that shows them what kind of life they can have. Of course, we had our movies, too. But, I don't know. It just isn't the same as having the parents get out and, now, why, you know, on a Sunday we had a team of horses. And we always, my dad put up ice, too, you know, so we could make ice cream. And we had this old cranker, you know, and we'd get the ice cream in the morning, get churning the ice cream. Mother would make the lunch and all that could get in the wagon, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we'd go clear up to Collins, what is that, four miles from Boulder?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: And we'd go way up there, you know, for the day. And we'd go as early as we could get ready, see, on Sunday. And Dad would unhook the team, you know, they could eat the grass. And Dad and maybe Wallace, the older ones, would go fishing and us kids would play if we didn't want to, if we were too little to fish or something. And then we'd come back and have our lunch and everything, go home, so time to milk the cow and go to bed. But that was a great treat to do something like that, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: For the amount that your father was working, was it unusual for him to be able to spend much time with the family?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yeah, it would be, it was, it was. So that was real special. It sounds like, it sounds like a pretty hard life for him too. It was, it was a very hard life. When I think of him clearing land and breaking that land and planting, and of all the gardening, you know, why you raised, practically raised all the potatoes we needed, you know. There for a few years we had wonderful crops around Bolton. Then as the timber is cut, it seems like the air currents, now it's freezing weather most of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it wasn't like that?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: No, why, you know, warmer, because of the timber, I'm sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Stocking the wind from blowing?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Uh huh, right.

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when your father, working as hard as he was, was it just to get by or was he really, was he really figuring that by doing this he was really getting somewhere?

speaker: Naomi Boll Parker
words: Well, he thought that he was, and like I say, he was one that, that didn't borrow for anything. See.
